retrieved_docs.title: Microdermabrasion -- Indications -- Transdermal Drug Delivery
retrieved_docs.content: Microdermabrasion has been shown to improve transdermal drug delivery by removing the stratum corneum. Methods to increase transdermal delivery often target the stratum corneum layer of the epidermis, as the stratum corneum is the principal barrier that limits the percutaneous diffusion of molecules. [10] Drugs diffuse more freely in the viable epidermis, directly below the stratum corneum layer. MDA has been shown to improve transdermal insulin delivery, transdermal vitamin C delivery, transdermal lidocaine delivery, and transdermal 5-fluorouracil delivery. [11] [12] [13] Although there are several ongoing clinical trials with promising results, the feasibility of using MDA in a clinical setting to enhance transdermal drug delivery is still unknown. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Lidocaine -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: An IV bolus of lidocaine has a very rapid onset of action. Lidocaine ointment provides local, topical anesthesia with an onset of action between 3 and 5 minutes. Absorption of lidocaine following topical application to mucous membranes varies based on the application site, concentration, duration, and dosage.

retrieved_docs.title: Lidocaine Toxicity(Archived) -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Potency correlates with lipid solubility, which is the ability of the local anesthetic molecule to penetrate membranes in a hydrophobic environment.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical, Local, and Regional Anesthesia and Anesthetics -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics block VGSCs, thereby preventing sodium influx into the cell and inhibiting impulse transmission. These agents are also classified as class I antiarrhythmic drugs due to their blockade of cardiac sodium channels, with lidocaine serving as the class IB prototype. They selectively block channels that frequently depolarize, as observed in tachyarrhythmias, thereby slowing transmission. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Microdermabrasion -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The primary clinical significance of microdermabrasion lies in cosmetic and aesthetic benefits. It is a minimally invasive procedure that can offer evened skin tone, bright complexion, reduced appearance of dark spots, and wrinkles, with cleaned-out pores and improved smoothness of the skin. Microdermabrasion has also been shown to enhance transdermal drug delivery by allowing the drug to diffuse more freely in the viable epidermis. The theoretical benefits of this are thought to include improved transdermal insulin delivery, transdermal vitamin C delivery, transdermal lidocaine delivery, and transdermal 5-fluorouracil delivery. Although there are several ongoing clinical trials with promising results, the feasibility of using microdermabrasion in a clinical setting to enhance transdermal drug delivery is still unknown.

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Treatment of Pigmented Lesions -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Local Anesthesia - Heat generation and tissue destruction, both hallmarks of laser therapy, can be painful. [25] One of the benefits of laser therapy for skin lesions is that their minimally invasive qualities allow for treatment to be done without general anesthesia. This opens up the possibility of in-office procedures and saves a trip to the operating room. In such cases, local anesthetics can be very useful, especially when children are being treated. Topical solutions such as EMLA cream (eutectic mixture of local anesthetics) and LMX (lidocaine 4% topical in lisosomal formula) can be used. Local nerve blocks may also be administered. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.content: For human in vivo penetration studies, tape stripping, or advanced spectroscopic methods, e.g., ATR (attenuated total reflection) spectroscopy is an option. A more advanced in vivo technique is microdialysis. [27] Some newer approaches include - principal components analysis (PCA), probabilistic analyses, fuzzy modeling, artificial neural network (ANN) modeling, and biopartitioning micellar chromatography, amongst others. [25] The development of newer carrier systems and techniques with nanoparticulation, ionto- and electroporation, liposomes, etc. have not only revolutionized the approach to enhance smooth percutaneous absorption of desirable ingredients but have correspondingly lead to the development of more sophisticated evaluation techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical, Local, and Regional Anesthesia and Anesthetics -- Mechanism of Action -- Clinical Applications and Safety
retrieved_docs.content: Emerging research in this field is focused on novel delivery systems and safety enhancements. A recent in vitro study described the development of pH-responsive local anesthetic formulations that automatically adjust their release based on tissue conditions, potentially revolutionizing their clinical applications if translatable in vivo. [13] This improved understanding of local anesthetic pharmacology continues to enhance patient care and safety in both surgical and pain management settings. The field remains dynamic, with ongoing research aimed at developing more targeted and efficient delivery systems while minimizing potential adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Transdermal Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Electroporation: Applying a high electrical voltage to the stratum corneum also creates small pores through which molecular substances can diffuse.

retrieved_docs.title: Lidocaine -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: An eutectic mixture of lidocaine and prilocaine (EMLA) combines the local anesthetics prilocaine and lidocaine. EMLA effectively penetrates the skin to provide local cutaneous anesthesia and is commonly used to avoid the pain associated with needle punctures.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical, Local, and Regional Anesthesia and Anesthetics -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: This blockade occurs in a use-dependent manner, which means that channels that are frequently active (eg, those in rapidly firing neurons or cardiac tissue experiencing tachyarrhythmia) are more susceptible to the blockade. A comprehensive review suggests that this selective targeting helps explain why local anesthetics are effective in treating both pain and cardiac arrhythmias while maintaining a favorable safety profile. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Lidocaine Toxicity(Archived) -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The duration of action also correlates with lipid solubility and protein binding. Highly lipid-soluble and protein-bound local anesthetics have a longer duration of action, presumably because they are less likely to be cleared by blood flow. Local anesthetics that are highly lipid-soluble also exhibit a high degree of plasma protein binding, mostly to alpha-1-acid glycoprotein and, to a lesser extent, albumin; consequently, their elimination is prolonged. Blood flow to the tissue deposit of local anesthetic determines the absorption rate and is responsible for the plasma level. It will, however, also transport local anesthetics away from the tissue site, reducing the risk of direct nerve toxicity. Lidocaine has a 90% hepatic metabolism, and the elimination half-life is 1.5 to 2 hours, which can be prolonged up to 3.5-fold in patients with severe liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- CO2 Ablative Lasers
retrieved_docs.content: Fractional ablative laser therapy was first available for CO2 lasers in 1998. [18] Fractional laser therapy uses narrow columns of laser light on the skin to create microscopic thermal zones, measuring less than 400 μm in diameter and up to 1300 μm in depth. This modality relies on smaller areas of ablation surrounded by viable tissue that permits rapid re-epithelization, as opposed to traditional laser therapy, which requires migration of epidermal cells. Fractional CO2 laser therapy possesses similar efficacy to traditional CO2 laser therapy, according to a number of studies that demonstrate up to 80% of patients report an acceptable reduction in rhytides. [18] Recovery is also more rapid with fractional therapy than with traditional CO2 laser therapy, with a return to normal activity after 5-10 days, depending on the treatment intensity. In addition, post-procedure topical hydroquinone cream, retinoids, or peeling agents may be prescribed to accelerate the resolution of erythema and edema. [11] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Iontophoresis Analgesic Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Transdermal iontophoresis of analgesics involves the use of an electromotive gradient (i.e., voltage) across a positively-charged anode and negatively-charged cathode to induce percutaneous infiltration of a therapeutic agent via active transport for local, regional, or systemic delivery (Figure 1). The primary interface medications must travel through is the stratum corneum (SC) of the skin, approximately 10 to 100 micrometers in thickness. [2] Three primary pathways exist for absorption through the SC: paracellular (between keratinocytes), transcellular (through keratinocytes), and appendageal (through hair follicles, sudoriferous glands, sebaceous glands, etc.) with the latter believed to have the lowest electromotive resistance. [2] [14] The quantification of percutaneous permeation is generally as a chemical flux (flow rate of the molecular or ionic agents over a given surface area). Employing electron-repulsion as a driving force, iontophoresis entails placing positively-ionized (cationic) or neutral medications below the positively-charged electrode (anode). In contrast, negatively-ionized drugs become oriented below the negatively-charged electrode (cathode) (Figure 1). Due to electrostatic repulsion, agents are driven away from their like-charged electrode(s) and through the integumentary interface. Numerous factors affecting medication flux and end-delivery have been elucidated and include the following [2] :

retrieved_docs.title: Ablative Laser Resurfacing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- CO2 Ablative Lasers
retrieved_docs.content: The discomfort associated with CO2 laser therapy often requires local anesthetic with additional sedation, anxiolytics, and possible oral analgesia, whereas a topical anesthetic alone is often sufficient for treatment with Er:YAG lasers. [9] After resurfacing with a CO2 laser, erythema will persist for approximately two weeks, for fractional treatments, or a period of several weeks to months for an aggressive non-fractional resurfacing. Re-epithelization of the epidermal layer following CO2 laser therapy occurs after approximately eight days. [13] [14] Studies have demonstrated improved clinical outcomes and greater dermal collagen remodeling with CO2 lasers compared with Er:YAG lasers on a per laser pass basis. [15] However, CO2 lasers are associated with a higher rate of dyspigmentation compared with Er:YAG lasers. [16] [17]  Photodamage is less severe in individuals with dark skin, but laser-induced dyspigmentation is a major concern for these individuals. For this reason, non-fractional CO2 laser use is not recommended for Fitzpatrick skin phototypes IV or higher. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetracaine -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians have administered tetracaine for various purposes since the early 1930s, but its most common use today is as a topical ophthalmic anesthetic for tonometry and other short-duration procedures on the surface of the eye, ear, and nose. Spinal anesthesia is another indication for IV tetracaine. A combination of lidocaine and tetracaine is used for topical local analgesia during superficial dermatological procedures, including pulsed dye laser therapy, dermal filler injection, laser-assisted tattoo removal, and facial laser resurfacing. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: The capsaicin 0.000325 mg/mg / lidocaine hydrochloride 0.04 mg/mg / menthol 0.1 mg/mg / methyl salicylate 0.275 mg/mg topical ointment (LidoPro) lists lidokain as its active moiety, indicating that lidokain provides the primary pharmacologic effect in this preparation. In contrast, the capsaicin 0.25 mg/mL / menthol 100 mg/mL / methyl salicylate 250 mg/mL topical lotion contains lidokain as an inactive ingredient, suggesting that lidokain is present only in a non‑therapeutic or sub‑therapeutic role in that formulation. Chemically, lidokain is derived from the parent compound phenylamide, placing it within the phenylamide class of local anesthetics. Recognizing that lidokain can function as either an active or inactive component depending on the specific topical product helps clinicians interpret the intended therapeutic purpose and potential effects of these formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 35
retrieved_docs.content: Lidokain (lidocaine) is a phenylamide classified as a local anesthetic and is placed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) class “Anesthetics for topical use,” a subclass of the broader antipruritic superclass that also contains agents such as benoxinate, pramoxine, tetrakain and benzydamin. Within this hierarchy, benoxinate is linked to the ophthalmologic local anesthetic prokain, and both are members of the Czech‑language category “anestetika lokální,” which subsumes local anesthetic throat preparations, neuropharmacologic agents and ophthalmologic anesthetics that share a sodium‑channel blockade mechanism. Lidokain serves as the active moiety in numerous combination topical products, including capsaicin‑containing ointments and patches (e.g., formulations with 0.000325 mg/mg capsaicin, 0.04 mg/mg lidocaine hydrochloride, menthol and methyl salicylate) and a medicated shampoo that also contains hydrocortisone and pramoxine for scalp irritation relief. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 32
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetics for topical use constitute a therapeutic class within the broader antipruritic category and include the agents cinchokain and lidokain. Both cinchokain and lidokain are also members of the more specific group “Local anesthetics for treatment of hemorrhoids and anal fissures for topical use,” which itself contains pramoxine and tetrakain, indicating shared clinical applications for hemorrhoidal disease, anal fissures, and skin irritation. Cinchokain is mapped from the synonym legrazol, while lidokain is chemically classified as a phenylamide. Lidokain serves as the active moiety in numerous combination topical formulations, such as capsaicin‑containing ointments and patches that also incorporate menthol and methyl salicylate (e.g., 0.000325 mg/mg capsaicin/0.04 mg/mg lidocaine hydrochloride/0.1 mg/mg menthol/0.275 mg/mg methyl salicylate ointment marketed as LidoPro, and similar products with slightly varying concentrations). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: as a pharmacologically active amino acid in certain systemic and injectable products and as a non‑active excipient in a broad range of topical dermatologic and cosmetic preparations, reflecting its versatile role in formulation science.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: Lidokain (lidocaine) is a phenylamide‑derived local anesthetic that is classified both as an anesthetic for topical use and as a local anesthetic for ophthalmologic applications. It is a core active moiety in numerous combination products designed for topical pain relief, including ointments, patches, lotions, and medicated shampoos. Formulations such as capsaicin/​lidocaine/​menthol/​methyl‑salicylate ointments and patches list lidokain as the active ingredient that provides local anesthesia, while a hydrocortisone/​lidocaine/​pramoxine medicated shampoo incorporates lidokain alongside other therapeutic agents. Although one product description lists lidokain as an inactive ingredient in a topical lotion, the prevailing role of lidokain across the listed preparations is that of an active anesthetic component. Within the broader category of topical anesthetics, benoxinate, pramoxine, tetrakain, and prokain are also members. Benoxinate is mapped from prokain, and both pramoxine and tetrakain are included in the subgroup of local anesthetics used for hemorrhoids and anal fissures. Additionally, the ophthalmologic local anesthetic group is linked to prokain, underscoring the overlapping classification of these agents. Overall, lidokain’s inclusion in diverse topical formulations and its membership in multiple anesthetic categories highlight its versatility as a local anesthetic for skin, mucosal, and ocular applications, and its relationship to other topical anesthetic agents such as benoxinate, pramoxine, tetrakain, and prokain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1586
retrieved_docs.content: Topical formulations that combine capsaicin, lidocaine (referred to as lidokain), menthol and methyl salicylate are marketed in several dosage forms, including ointments, medicated patches and lotions. In the capsaicin‑containing ointments (e.g., the 0.000325 mg/mg preparation and the 0.000375 mg/mg preparation) lidokain is identified as the active moiety, while in a medicated patch (0.00035 mg/mg capsaicin with 0.005 mg/mg lidocaine) lidokain is listed as an active ingredient alongside capsaicin, indicating that both agents contribute to the therapeutic effect. A topical lotion that contains a higher concentration of capsaicin (0.25 mg/mL) lists lidokain as an inactive ingredient, illustrating that the same compound can serve different roles depending on the formulation. Capsaicin (spelled “kapsaicin” in some records) is repeatedly recorded as an ingredient and, in certain products, as an active ingredient, underscoring its central role in the analgesic and counter‑irritant properties of these preparations. Both lidokain and capsaisin belong to the phenylamide chemical class, as each is described as having phenylamide as a parent structure. Capsicum, the plant source of capsaicin, is associated with kapsaicin, and a phytonutrient termed “fytonutrienty” is also linked to kapsaicin, reflecting the broader nutritional and pharmacological relevance of this phenylamide derivative. Together, these relationships highlight how phenylamide‑based compounds are integrated into multi‑component topical products to provide analgesia, counter‑irritation, and enhanced patient comfort.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 578
retrieved_docs.content: Lidokain (lidocaine) is a phenylamide derivative that serves as the active moiety in numerous topical preparations, including the LidoPro ointment (capsaicin 0.000325 mg/mg / lidokain 0.04 mg/mg / menthol 0.1 mg/mg / methyl‑salicylate 0.275 mg/mg), a similar ointment with slightly different concentrations, a medicated patch (lidokain 0.00035 mg/mg / capsaicin 0.005 mg/mg), and a hydrocortisone 0.5 mg/mL / lidokain 0.5 mg/mL / pramoxine 2.5 mg/mL shampoo. In each product lidokain provides local anesthetic action. Capsaicin (also spelled kapsaicin) is a phenylamide derived from plants of the genus Capsicum and from the Solanaceae alkaloid family; it is classified as a biological toxin (biologické toxiny). Capsaicin contributes analgesic, counter‑irritant and sensitizing effects and is present in many of the same formulations as lidokain, as well as in stand‑alone lotions such as capsaicin 0.25 mg/mL / menthol 100 mg/mL / methyl‑salicylate 300 mg/mL (commercially referenced as Dendracin Neurodendraxcin) and in benzokain, which also contains menthol and methyl‑salicylate and is linked to the synonym “fastins.” Menthol adds a cooling sensation, while methyl‑salicylate provides additional counter‑irritant activity. The knowledge base emphasizes that lidokain and capsaicin belong to the same phenylamide chemical family, are frequently combined to achieve synergistic local pain relief, and originate from natural sources; recognizing capsaicin’s status as a biological toxin informs safety considerations, dosing, and monitoring when these combination products are used for localized analgesia and anti‑inflammatory therapy.

retrieved_docs.content: Cinchokain is classified as a member of the topical anesthetic group, indicating its use as a local anesthetic applied to the skin or mucous membranes. The compound cinchokain is mapped from legrazol, suggesting that legrazol is the source, precursor, or synonymous entity from which cinchokain is derived or identified. Consequently, legrazol is closely related to cinchokain within the context of anesthetics for topical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 44
retrieved_docs.content: Lidocaine (lidokain) is a phenylamide‑derived local anesthetic that is classified within the broad Czech class “anestetika lokální” (local anesthetic agents) and appears in several more specific subgroups, including “Anesthetics for topical use,” “Local anesthetics for treatment of hemorrhoids and anal fissures for topical use,” “Local anesthetic throat preparations,” and “Local anesthetics, ophthalmologic.” Within the “anestetika lokální” hierarchy lidocaine is grouped with agents such as pramoxine, tetrakain, prokain, benoxinate, and dyclonine, reflecting a shared therapeutic purpose of providing localized numbness across mucosal, ocular, anorectal, and throat surfaces. Prokain itself is linked to the ophthalmologic subset, while benoxinate is mapped from prokain as an inverse subclass. The product “Local Anesthetic [EPC]” is associated with the throat‑preparation subgroup, illustrating the clinical relevance of this classification. In pharmaceutical formulations lidocaine is consistently the active moiety, often supplied as lidocaine hydrochloride. It is incorporated into multimodal topical preparations that combine it with capsaicin, menthol, methyl salicylate, hydrocortisone, and pramoxine, delivering concentrations from 0.005 mg/mg to 0.04 mg/mg for conditions such as hemorrhoids, anal fissures, throat irritation, and ocular procedures. Although a capsaicin‑menthol‑methyl salicylate lotion once listed lidocaine as an “inactive ingredient,” its pharmacologic role remains that of an active local anesthetic, resolving the apparent discrepancy. Overall, the community’s knowledge depicts lidocaine as a central component of diverse topical therapies, situated within a hierarchical framework of local anesthetic agents that spans ophthalmologic, throat, neuropharmacologic, and anorectal applications, and frequently combined with other analgesic or anti‑inflammatory agents to enhance patient‑care outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2805
retrieved_docs.content: The topical formulations described contain capsaicin, menthol, methyl salicylate and varying concentrations of lidokain (lidocaine). In two capsaicin‑based topical ointments—one with 0.000325 mg/mg lidocaine hydrochloride and another with 0.000375 mg/mg lidocaine—lidokain is identified as the active moiety or active ingredient, indicating that it provides the primary pharmacologic effect of these products. In a separate capsaicin‑based topical lotion (0.25 mg/mL capsaicin, 100 mg/mL menthol, 250 mg/mL methyl salicylate), lidokain is listed as an inactive ingredient, which reflects an inconsistency in labeling because lidokain is typically an active anesthetic agent. This discrepancy suggests that the lotion’s ingredient classification may be erroneous or that lidokain is present in a non‑therapeutic role in that specific formulation. Additionally, lidokain is classified within the phenylamide chemical family, indicating that phenylamide is its parent compound. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians recognize lidokain’s role as an active anesthetic in most capsaicin‑containing topical products, while also being aware of potential labeling variations that could affect prescribing and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1143
retrieved_docs.content: Lidokain (lidocaine) is a phenylamide derivative that functions as a class Ib antiarrhythmic agent; it shares this pharmacologic class with mexiletine, another class Ib antiarrhythmic. In addition to its systemic cardiac effects, lidokain serves as the active moiety or active ingredient in a variety of topical combination products. These include a capsaicin‑0.000325 mg/mg / lidocaine hydrochloride‑0.04 mg/mg / menthol‑0.1 mg/mg / methyl‑salicylate‑0.275 mg/mg topical ointment (also marketed as LidoPro), a capsaicin‑0.00035 mg/mg / lidocaine hydrochloride‑0.005 mg/mg / menthol‑0.05 mg/mg / methyl‑salicylate‑0.2 mg/mg medicated patch, and a capsaicin‑0.000375 mg/mg / lidocaine‑0.04 mg/mg / menthol‑0.1 mg/mg / methyl‑salicylate‑0.2 mg/mg topical ointment. In each of these formulations lidokain provides local anesthetic activity that complements the analgesic and counter‑irritant effects of capsaicin, menthol, and methyl‑salicylate. A listed capsaicin‑0.25 mg/mL / menthol‑100 mg/mL / methyl‑salicylate‑250 mg/mL topical lotion erroneously identified lidokain as an inactive ingredient; given lidokain’s well‑established role as a local anesthetic, it is more accurately regarded as an active component in such products. Collectively, these relationships illustrate lidokain’s dual relevance in clinical practice: as a systemic class Ib antiarrhythmic for cardiac rhythm management and as a key active ingredient in multi‑component topical preparations used for pain relief and skin irritation.

Lidokain serves as the active moiety in numerous combination topical products, including low‑dose capsaicin‑lidocaine ointments (e.g., 0.000325 mg/mg lidocaine hydrochloride), a medicated patch (0.005 mg/mg lidocaine hydrochloride), and formulations that pair lidokain with capsaicin, menthol or methyl salicylate.
